questions.question: Explain the formation of coal and name its different ranks.
keyPoints: Coal is formed from remains of ancient plants through carbonization over millions of years
Process: Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite
Anthracite has highest carbon content (86-97%) and calorific value

questions.question: Describe the working of a thermal power plant with a neat diagram.
keyPoints: Main components: Boiler, Turbine, Generator, Condenser
Working: Fuel burned in boiler → produces steam → steam drives turbine → turbine spins generator → electricity generated
Steam condensed in condenser and recycled back to boiler

questions.question: What are the environmental impacts of using fossil fuels?
keyPoints: Air pollution: Release of CO, CO2, SO2, NOx
Greenhouse effect and global warming due to CO2
Acid rain from SO2 and NOx
Water pollution from oil spills and mining
Health hazards from particulate matter

questions.question: Define calorific value and state the calorific value of natural gas.
keyPoints: Calorific value is the amount of heat energy produced by complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel
Natural gas calorific value: 38,000-56,000 kJ/kg (highest among fossil fuels)
Unit: kJ/kg or kcal/kg

questions.question: Explain cogeneration in power plants.
keyPoints: Cogeneration or CHP (Combined Heat and Power)
Simultaneous production of electricity and useful heat from single fuel source
Overall efficiency: 60-80% (compared to 30-40% for conventional plants
Applications: District heating, industries, hospitals

questions.question: What is hydroelectric power and how is it generated?
keyPoints: Hydroelectric power uses kinetic energy of falling/flowing water
Components: Dam, Reservoir, Penstock, Turbine, Generator
Water from height flows through penstock → spins turbine → generator produces electricity
Renewable energy source replenished by rain cycle

questions.question: Explain the Carnot efficiency formula and its significance.
keyPoints: Carnot efficiency: η = 1 - (Tc/Th)
Tc = absolute temperature of cold reservoir, Th = absolute temperature of hot reservoir
Represents maximum theoretical efficiency of a heat engine
Higher temperature difference → higher efficiency
Practical plants cannot achieve 100% due to this limit
